title: Research: can @pure functions access thread-local or global mutable state?
description: The @pure attribute is described as telling the compiler 'trust me, this has no effects.' The spec gives the memoization cache as an example. This implies @pure functions CAN access global mutable state (via Mutex or atomic ops) but claim purity anyway. This has two sub-questions: (1) Is the @pure claim only about the effect system, or does it also mean 'no observable side effects from the caller's perspective'? (2) Can an interrupt handler in a no-alloc context call a @pure function that internally allocates via its hidden cache? Determine what @pure actually promises, document it precisely, and decide whether that promise is strong enough to be useful.
close_reason: Added §2.4 subsections: (1) thread-local mutable state generates ! IO — same rule as process-global state, not suppressible by @pure, with example. (2) @pure and no-alloc contexts — documented acknowledged limitation: @pure is a correctness claim not a resource-usage claim; no-alloc callers must audit @pure callees manually.
